source_pr.title: ddl: fix rule cache panic when truncating partitions
source_pr.body:
### What problem does this PR solve?

Problem Summary: `newIDs[i]` will panic, due to this [commit](https://github.com/pingcap/tidb/pull/20622/commits/4417a586249882c2dd135e5ba25df2870f7b18ca). That commit moved the definition of `newIDs`, and now `len(newIDs) == 0` in the loop `for i, oldID := range oldIDs`. Thus `newIDs[i]` will panic.

We should not get ID from `newIDs`, and we should get it from `newPartitions[i].ID`.

### Check List <!--REMOVE the items that are not applicable-->

Tests <!-- At least one of them must be included. -->

- Unit test
- Integration test
- Manual test (add detailed scripts or steps below)

Refer to manual tests in #19505.

### Release note <!-- bugfixes or new feature need a release note -->

- Fix panic when truncating partitions with placement rules

symptoms: sort.Slice(partDefs, func(i, j int) bool { return partDefs[i].Name.L < partDefs[j].Name.L }) (ddl/placement_sql_test.go)
failure_signature: sort.Slice(partDefs, func(i, j int) bool { return partDefs[i].Name.L < partDefs[j].Name.L }) (ddl/placement_sql_test.go)
root_cause_explanation: The test was flaky around DDL/schema changes where schema propagation is asynchronous. This PR stabilizes it by: sort.Slice(partDefs, func(i, j int) bool { return partDefs[i].Name.L < partDefs[j].Name.L }) (ddl/placement_sql_test.go).
fix_pattern: sort.Slice(partDefs, func(i, j int) bool { return partDefs[i].Name.L < partDefs[j].Name.L }) (ddl/placement_sql_test.go)

evidence: evidence: sort.Slice(partDefs, func(i, j int) bool { return partDefs[i].Name.L < partDefs[j].Name.L }) (ddl/placement_sql_test.go)
